title: Hardcoded credential in C# source
summary: A password, API key, token, or connection string is hardcoded as a string literal in C# source. Hardcoded secrets are visible to anyone with access to the source, cannot be rotated without a code change, and are frequently exposed in version control history.
why_it_matters: Hardcoded credentials are one of the most common causes of real-world breaches. Once a secret is committed to source control, it is effectively compromised — even if you delete the file, the history retains the secret. Public GitHub repositories are scanned by automated bots within minutes of a push, and secrets are actively weaponized. For internal repositories, any developer, contractor, or CI system with read access has the secret.
how_to_fix:
Move secrets out of source and load them at runtime from a secure source:

1. For local development: use `dotnet user-secrets` and reference them via IConfiguration.
2. For production: use Azure Key Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or environment variables managed by your deployment platform.
3. Load secrets via IConfiguration at startup: `builder.Configuration["MySecret"]` — the same code works in dev, staging, and prod because each environment points IConfiguration at a different source.
4. Never commit `appsettings.Production.json` or similar files with real values — use `appsettings.Template.json` with placeholder values and document the required settings.
5. If you find a secret in source history, rotate it immediately. Removing the commit is not enough — assume the secret is compromised the moment it was pushed.
developer_notes: String.Empty, "changeme", "TODO", and similar placeholder values will not trigger this rule. If you legitimately need a constant credential for test fixtures, prefix it with `test_` or mark it with a `// nosemgrep:` pragma. Be careful with `const string` — these are baked into compiled DLLs and are trivial to extract even from obfuscated assemblies.
verification_checklist: No string literals matching password/apikey/token/secret field names in source
No high-entropy strings with known prefixes (ghp_, sk_live_, AKIA, etc.) in source
All secrets are loaded via IConfiguration, Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable, or a secrets manager
Test fixtures use clearly-labeled placeholder values or load from a separate test config
Git history has been scanned for leaked secrets (e.g., with gitleaks or trufflehog)
Any secrets found in history have been rotated

references.title: Safe storage of app secrets in development in ASP.NET Core
